body:
EU Digital Asset Radar tracks the European Union's digital-asset regulatory framework at EU level -- the Regulations and Directives themselves, European Commission delegated and implementing acts, and the technical standards, guidelines and coordination work of ESMA, EBA, the ECB and AMLA. This page is a plain-English map to get oriented before exploring the pillar boards, the trajectory board, or the document library. Every claim on this site traces to a primary source; this page is a summary of that underlying record, not a substitute for reading the sources themselves.

A scope limitation before anything else: this radar deliberately does not track the 27 member states' own national competent authorities (NCAs). Under the Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA), it is each member state's NCA -- not any EU-level body -- that actually authorises a crypto-asset service provider (CASP) or a token issuer, supervises it day to day, and takes enforcement action against it by name. Those are national administrative acts, made under national procedure, and they are out of scope of every pillar on this site. A reader who wants to know whether a specific firm is authorised should consult ESMA's public register of authorised CASPs and issuers, and the relevant NCA directly -- not this site.

Who regulates what at EU level

Five bodies do the EU-level work this radar follows. The European Commission proposes and adopts legislation -- MiCA itself, its delegated and implementing acts, and the review clauses that will reshape it over time -- and publishes it, together with all other adopted EU law, through EUR-Lex, the official publication point this site cites for legal text but does not itself watch by feed. The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) covers the securities-markets side of MiCA: technical standards and guidelines for crypto-asset service providers (trading platforms, custody, execution, advice and the rest), the market-abuse regime for crypto-assets, the EU-wide interim register of authorised CASPs and issuers, and coordination among NCAs. The European Banking Authority (EBA) covers the banking side: technical standards and guidelines for issuers of asset-referenced tokens (ARTs) and e-money tokens (EMTs), and -- uniquely among these bodies -- direct EU-level supervision of ARTs and EMTs that are designated significant, making EBA the one genuine EU-level supervisory line under MiCA rather than a standard-setting or coordinating one. The European Central Bank (ECB) engages through the digital euro project, formal opinions on EU legislative proposals, and its banking-supervision interest in significant banks' crypto-asset exposures; this site's watcher follows the ECB's own press-and-speeches RSS feed, which was confirmed live during this research. The Authority for Anti-Money Laundering and Countering the Financing of Terrorism (AMLA), seated in Frankfurt and operational since 1 July 2025, is currently coordinating AML/CFT supervisory convergence and building out the selection process for the obliged entities -- potentially including some CASPs -- that it will supervise directly from a later date under its own statute.

What is already in force

The defining fact about the EU's regime, in contrast to jurisdictions still anticipating their first cryptoasset-specific law, is that MiCA is already fully applicable. Titles III and IV, governing ARTs and EMTs, applied from 30 June 2024. The rest of the Regulation -- including CASP authorisation under Title V and the crypto-asset market-abuse regime under Title VI -- applied from 30 December 2024. CASPs authorised under national law before that date could continue trading under a transitional regime, but its outer limit expired on 1 July 2026, after which ESMA and NCAs began coordinating the wind-down of any CASP that remained unauthorised. Alongside MiCA, the recast Transfer of Funds Regulation extended the AML travel rule to crypto-asset transfers from the same 30 December 2024 date, CRR3's transitional prudential treatment of banks' own crypto-asset exposures has applied since 9 July 2024, and the narrower DLT Pilot Regime for tokenised-securities market infrastructure has applied since March 2023. The current phase for all of this is implementation, not anticipation: finishing the remaining regulatory and implementing technical standards, working through EBA's significance-designation process for ARTs and EMTs, and supervising firms that are already authorised and trading.

What is coming next

The genuinely forward-looking items are fewer and later-dated. The single EU AML Regulation and the sixth AML Directive, both adopted in 2024, apply and must be transposed respectively from 10 July 2027; AMLA's own direct supervision of a first cohort of selected obliged entities does not begin until 2028, with the selection process running through 2027. The digital euro remains a European Commission proposal, not yet agreed law -- the Council and Parliament have each adopted negotiating positions and entered trilogue negotiations, but no final text or issuance date exists.

How to use this site

The pillar boards give the current EU-level state of each of the site's seven regulatory areas in one paragraph each. The trajectory board lists officially announced upcoming dates in one place. The document library indexes every primary-source document the site's automated watcher has observed. The glossary defines the jargon used throughout, and terms shared with other jurisdictions link to a single cross-jurisdiction entry. Every summary card cites the specific primary source it is drawn from.

AI-generated summary for general information. Not legal or regulatory advice. Always verify against the linked primary source.
